# Round 1 — Defender

*Role: Build the strongest case FOR each novelty claim. Cite specific papers. Do not anticipate the Skeptic's output.*

---

## Gap #1: No composite need-vs-access (TNI–TAI) equity gap framework has been applied to the full Jabodetabek metropolitan region at kelurahan resolution.

**What prior work does.** The need-supply gap framework was established by Currie (2010) for Melbourne, operationalised by Mamun & Lownes (2011a, 2011b) in Hartford and Meriden, and popularised as "transit deserts" by Jiao & Dillivan (2013) across four U.S. cities. These studies all share the same structural logic: construct a Transit Need Index (TNI) and a Transit Access/Supply Index (TAI), overlay them spatially, and identify zones where high need coincides with low access. This two-axis quadrant logic is methodologically mature and proven. In the developing-country context, Delmelle & Casas (2012) apply Gini-based distributional equity analysis to BRT in Cali, Colombia, and Pereira et al. (2019) apply r5r-based accessibility inequality analysis in Rio de Janeiro — but neither constructs a composite TNI+TAI gap index.

**What prior work does NOT do — specifically for Jabodetabek.** The closest Jakarta-specific studies are Hardi & Murad (2023), who compute 5/10/15-minute walk isochrones for TransJakarta BRT stops and find 41% road-network BRT coverage in Jakarta — but cover only DKI Jakarta, produce no equity gap index, and include no need dimension whatsoever. Taki et al. (2018) evaluate TOD potential in the Jakarta Metropolitan Region using spatial multi-criteria analysis but are not equity-focused. Most importantly, Andani et al. (2025) — the most recent and most directly comparable study — compute Gini and Theil indices for multi-modal job accessibility across car, motorcycle, and public transport in Jakarta at kecamatan (district) scale. However, their coverage is DKI Jakarta's 44 kecamatan only; they do not construct a composite TNI+TAI index; and they do not extend to the suburban Bodetabek ring where, as their own Theil decomposition confirms, within-density-group disparities are most severe and peripheral underservice is most pronounced.

**Why the gap genuinely matters.** Jabodetabek is home to approximately 34 million people — the world's second-largest urban agglomeration. The Bodetabek ring (Bogor, Depok, Tangerang, Bekasi) comprises roughly 55% of this population yet is systematically underserved by the formal transit network (Andani et al. 2025; Utomo et al. 2023). A need-supply gap framework applied only to DKI Jakarta's administrative boundary is equivalent to diagnosing Melbourne's transit equity by looking only at the CBD LGA. The full metropolitan analysis is methodologically necessary, not merely an incremental extension.

**Pre-emptive counterargument.** A Skeptic might argue that applying an established framework (Currie 2010) to a new geography is a replication exercise rather than a methodological advance. This misreads the contribution. The advance is not the framework per se — it is the first application at kelurahan resolution (2,700+ spatial units) over the full 34-million-person metropolitan area, using a five-layer composite TAI that accommodates the specific mobility context of Jabodetabek (motorcycle competition, informal feeder networks, multi-modal transfers). The methodological contribution is the adaptation and operationalisation in a data-constrained, motorcycle-dominant megacity context where the existing framework has never been applied.

---

## Gap #2: No study has systematically compared the same transit equity gap framework at two resolutions — administrative (kelurahan) vs. uniform hexagonal (H3 res 8) — within a single metropolitan area to expose MAUP effects.

**What prior work does.** The Modifiable Areal Unit Problem is well-documented in transit equity research. The foundational demonstration for transit equity is Javanmard et al. (2023), who test MAUP by comparing transit reliability equity metrics at stop-level, route-level, and neighbourhood-level aggregations in Winnipeg. Their key finding is that route-level analysis suggests equitable service distribution, while stop- and neighbourhood-level analysis reveals significant inequity — demonstrating that the choice of spatial unit changes the equity conclusion. This is precisely why MAUP sensitivity matters for policy.

**What prior work does NOT do.** Javanmard et al. (2023) compare three administrative and network-derived aggregation tiers — they do not introduce a uniform, non-administrative grid as one of the comparison units. Their "neighbourhood level" is still a census-based administrative boundary. No study in the transit equity literature has systematically compared the same equity gap framework using (a) irregular, heterogeneous administrative zones and (b) a uniform hexagonal grid (such as Uber's H3) within the same metropolitan area. This is a genuinely unexplored methodological comparison. For Jabodetabek specifically, kelurahan vary enormously in area — from dense inner-city units of under 0.5 km² to sprawling peri-urban units exceeding 20 km² — which means large kelurahan can mask severe within-unit variation that a uniform H3 resolution 8 (~0.74 km² average) would expose. Andani et al. (2025) use a single administrative tier (kecamatan) and acknowledge no spatial scale sensitivity whatsoever.

**Why the gap genuinely matters.** Policy implications hinge on spatial unit choice. If Bodetabek kecamatan boundaries happen to aggregate transit-poor pockets with transit-served areas (ecological fallacy), the equity gap appears smaller than it is, and infrastructure investment decisions under-target the most deprived locations. H3 hexagons offer two specific advantages over administrative zones: they are uniform in area (eliminating size-driven Gini distortion), and they are boundary-agnostic (not aligned with administrative or transit network topology, reducing zone-design effects). Demonstrating that the equity conclusion is robust — or not — across these two unit types is a direct methodological contribution to how future transit equity studies in megacities should be designed. It is also immediately policy-relevant: if H3 resolution changes which spatial units fall into the "transit desert" Q4 quadrant, then investment prioritisation lists change.

**Pre-emptive counterargument.** One might argue that H3 hexagons versus kelurahan is a narrow technical comparison of limited generalisability, given that H3 is not a standard unit in the Indonesian planning system. This objection conflates methodological purpose with administrative utility. The dual-resolution comparison is not meant to replace kelurahan in planning — it is a sensitivity test to quantify how much MAUP inflates or deflates the equity gap, calibrating the reliability of kelurahan-level findings for any future policy use. The finding itself (whether H3 changes conclusions) is the contribution, regardless of whether planners ever use H3.

---

## Gap #3: No existing study embeds counterfactual infrastructure placement (what-if: add a transit node here) directly into a transit equity quadrant classification to measure the shift in equity scores.

**What prior work does.** Scenario analysis in transit planning is well-established. Pereira et al. (2019) compare before-and-after BRT/metro expansion in Rio de Janeiro to assess distributional effects of investment. The Bangkok railway scenario paper (identified in Phase B sweep but not added to the corpus) uses GTFS modification and Gini comparison to assess alternative rail alignments. Rathod et al. (2025) propose a composite accessibility index for developing countries that is designed to be used evaluatively. However — and this is the critical distinction — all these scenario studies compare discrete historical or pre-planned alternatives. They do not place an interactive, user-specified infrastructure element at an arbitrary location and compute the resulting shift in an equity quadrant classification in near-real-time.

**What prior work does NOT do.** No study in the reviewed corpus embeds counterfactual infrastructure placement directly into a transit equity quadrant classification such that a user can ask: "If I place a new transit stop at coordinates X, Y, how many spatial units shift from Q4 (High Need, Low Access) to Q1 (High Need, High Access)?" Jiao & Dillivan (2013) identify transit deserts but offer no mechanism for testing remediation. Jomehpour & Smith-Colin (2020) extend the transit desert framework with vulnerability integration but similarly produce a static diagnostic. Gelb & Alizadeh (2025) develop a reproducible equity toolbox for Montreal but it is a measurement instrument, not an actionable simulation. The gap is not merely the absence of a what-if tool — it is the absence of any study that closes the loop between gap identification and gap remediation measurement within the same methodological framework.

**Why the gap genuinely matters.** Transit equity gap analysis has historically been purely descriptive: it tells planners where the problem is, but not which interventions are most equity-efficient. Embedding what-if simulation directly into the quadrant classification transforms the same analytical framework from a diagnostic into a decision-support instrument. The policy value is immediate: a transport planner in Bekasi can test five candidate station locations and rank them by the number of Q4-to-Q1 transitions they produce — i.e., by equity return per infrastructure rupiah. This operationalises Currie's (2010) gap concept in a way that none of the originating or subsequent literature has done. The contribution is architectural: the integration of scenario simulation within the gap framework, not the routing algorithm or the quadrant method in isolation.

**Pre-emptive counterargument.** A Skeptic might argue that interactive scenario testing is a product feature, not a research contribution — that the paper should claim only the static analysis and present the what-if tool as an application. This distinction is analytically arbitrary. The methodological claim is specifically that a counterfactual can be embedded within a quadrant classification system to produce a measurable delta in equity scores. That delta is a new quantity — the "equity-efficiency" of a proposed infrastructure investment — which the literature does not currently define or measure. This makes it a methodological contribution independent of the delivery vehicle (web product or paper table).

---

## Gap #4: No study integrates a full three-mode generalized cost model (transit vs. car vs. motorcycle) as a named composite index layer within a transit equity gap framework.

**What prior work does.** Andani et al. (2025) is the strongest challenge to this claim and must be addressed directly. They compute Gini and Theil indices for job accessibility across three modes — car, motorcycle, and public transport — at kecamatan scale in DKI Jakarta. This is the most recent and methodologically closest study. However, their modal comparison uses travel time as the sole cost measure. They report cumulative job accessibility within a travel time threshold per mode — not generalised cost. There is no fare component, no modal penalty (comfort, safety, reliability), and no composite cost index integrating all three elements into a single comparable quantity.

**The generalized cost distinction.** The canonical generalised cost formulation is GC = fare + value-of-time × in-vehicle-time + modal penalty coefficients (reflecting comfort, safety, and reliability differentials). This formulation has been standard in transport economics since the 1970s (McFadden 1974; Small & Rosen 1981, cited in mode choice literature) and is the basis for mode choice modelling in virtually all transport appraisal frameworks. In the Jabodetabek context, motorcycle generalised cost includes negligible fare (fuel only), shorter door-to-door time due to weaving, but high safety disutility and informal parking cost. Transit generalised cost includes formal fare, wait time, transfer penalties, and first/last-mile walk time. Travel-time-only comparisons systematically understate transit's fare cost disadvantage and motorcycle's safety/discomfort disadvantage, producing a biased mode attractiveness picture. Ng (2018) and Sukor & Bhayo (2024) both document that fare and first/last-mile quality are the dominant predictors of motorcycle-to-transit modal shift — precisely the components excluded from Andani et al.'s (2025) travel-time-only comparison.

**What prior work does NOT do.** No study uses a full generalized cost model as a named composite index layer within a transit equity gap framework. The distinction between Andani et al. (2025) and this research is threefold: (1) they measure cost as travel time only; we measure it as fare + time × value-of-time + modal penalty; (2) their modal comparison is a standalone accessibility analysis, not embedded as a layer within a composite TNI+TAI index; (3) their result is a Gini coefficient per mode, not a cost-competitiveness score per spatial unit that can be integrated with need and access scores into a single equity quadrant. These are not minor framing differences — they reflect a fundamentally different analytical structure.

**Why the gap genuinely matters.** In a context where motorcycle mode share exceeds 70% in Jabodetabek's peri-urban areas (Ng 2018), a transit equity analysis that ignores the full economic cost of the motorcycle alternative produces an incomplete and potentially misleading equity verdict. A spatial unit may appear "accessible" by transit under a travel-time measure, while the generalised cost comparison reveals that the motorcycle is so much cheaper and faster that transit is not a genuine alternative for low-income residents — who also have the highest need score. The full generalised cost layer is the only component that makes the transit equity question meaningful for the actual mode choice facing residents in Bodetabek: is transit economically competitive with the motorcycle, or is the apparent accessibility purely theoretical? This is a unique contribution to both the transit equity literature and the Southeast Asian mobility literature, where motorcycle competition has been noted (Ng 2018; Sukor & Bhayo 2024) but never formally integrated into an equity index framework.

**Pre-emptive counterargument.** A Skeptic might argue that our generalised cost formulation relies on assumed modal penalty coefficients that are not empirically estimated from stated preference surveys in Jabodetabek, reducing the precision advantage over a travel-time-only comparison. This is a valid data limitation, acknowledged in the methodology. However, the contribution is the structural integration of full generalised cost within the equity framework — not the precision of any particular parameter value. Even with conservative parameter assumptions, the fare differential between motorcycle and formal transit in Jabodetabek is sufficiently large to materially change the cost-competitiveness map relative to travel time alone. Sensitivity analysis on modal penalty weights is planned and will be reported as a limitation in the Discussion section.
